description: HelloMavens-authored mappings from each SBS control (54 at upstream main @ d4304e1) to OWASP Top 10 2021 categories, regulatory citations (HIPAA Security Rule, SOC 2 Trust Services Criteria 2017, ISO 27001:2022 Annex A, GDPR, CCPA), a `cli_evidence_class` flag (Phase 5 Block E) tagging which controls have a verified CLI evidence path, and a `pass_narrative` (Phase 6 / appendix validation cards) — a 20-40 word customer-facing string describing what passing the control protects against. Merged into hellomavens_enrichments by scripts/sync-sbs.ts. See control-enrichments.RATIONALE.md for the editorial reasoning per category.

sources.ccpa: California Consumer Privacy Act, Cal. Civ. Code §§1798.100-199.100

SBS-ACS-001.pass_narrative: Your permission set model is documented and enforced, so privilege sprawl can't accumulate quietly — every authorization construct conforms to the standard, and access reviews actually have something to compare against.

SBS-ACS-002.pass_narrative: Your API-enabled authorizations are tracked with documented justification, so you can see who has programmatic data access at a glance and prove every permission is intentional during audit reviews.

SBS-ACS-003.pass_narrative: This high-risk permission is granted only to documented, justified users, so people can't quietly self-authorize OAuth apps that bypass your Connected App governance and reach data without oversight.

SBS-ACS-004.pass_narrative: Your super-admin–equivalent users are documented and tracked, so investigations are faster, access reviews are credible, and orphaned admin accounts can't accumulate quietly behind the scenes.

SBS-ACS-005.pass_narrative: Regular users sit on custom profiles, not Salesforce-managed standard ones, so platform updates can't enable new permissions without your approval and least privilege is actually enforceable.

SBS-ACS-006.pass_narrative: This permission, which bypasses API Access Control entirely, is granted only to justified users, so external apps can't get data access without your vetting and allowlisting first.

SBS-ACS-007.pass_narrative: You have a current inventory of non-human identities, so automated access can be reviewed, orphaned credentials can be retired, and security incidents involving integrations are actually investigable.

SBS-ACS-008.pass_narrative: Non-human identities aren't carrying broad privileges by default, so a compromised API key or OAuth token can't bypass sharing rules or trigger administrative operations org-wide.

SBS-ACS-009.pass_narrative: Privileged non-human identities have compensating controls layered on top of credentials, so secret leakage isn't a single point of failure for your most sensitive integrations.

SBS-ACS-010.pass_narrative: Access is reviewed and recertified on a cadence, so privilege creep can't quietly accumulate from old roles, temporary projects, and forgotten grants — least privilege stays current over time.

SBS-ACS-011.pass_narrative: Access changes go through governed approval and audit, so excessive permissions can't slip in unnoticed and incident investigations have a paper trail to follow.

SBS-ACS-012.pass_narrative: Privileged accounts are classified for login-hours restrictions, adding a defense-in-depth layer — so even a compromised credential can't be exploited freely during off-hours when detection is thin.

SBS-AUTH-001.pass_narrative: SSO enforcement is on org-wide, so users can't quietly authenticate around centralized identity management — credential-based attacks lose their direct path into your org.

SBS-AUTH-002.pass_narrative: SSO bypass exceptions are documented and approved, so off-SSO accounts can't proliferate unnoticed and audit reviews have clean visibility into every authentication exception.

SBS-AUTH-003.pass_narrative: Profile login IP ranges are scoped, not wide-open, so authentication can't happen from arbitrary locations on the internet — a defense-in-depth layer if a credential ever leaks.

SBS-AUTH-004.pass_narrative: MFA is enforced for external users with substantial data access, so a stolen password alone can't sign someone in — phishing and credential-stuffing lose their main path to your sensitive data.

SBS-CODE-001.pass_narrative: Every Salesforce code change goes through peer review before deployment, so a single developer — compromised, malicious, or simply mistaken — can't push insecure code into production unilaterally.

SBS-CODE-002.pass_narrative: Static code analysis runs before every merge, so SOQL injection, insecure data exposure, and improper access control patterns get caught at code-review time instead of in production.

SBS-CODE-003.pass_narrative: Persistent application logging is wired in, so security investigations actually have data to work with — Salesforce's transient debug logs aren't your only forensic surface.

SBS-CODE-004.pass_narrative: Sensitive data is kept out of application logs, so a low-privilege account with log access can't extract credentials or PII without tripping the access controls on the source objects.

SBS-CPORTAL-001.pass_narrative: Portal Apex methods don't accept user-controlled parameters for record access, so external users can't manipulate inputs to enumerate records they shouldn't see.

SBS-CPORTAL-002.pass_narrative: Guest users have minimal record access, so unauthenticated traffic can't quietly enumerate organizational data through your customer-portal trust boundary.

SBS-DATA-001.pass_narrative: You can detect regulated data in long-text fields, so PII can't accumulate in unknown locations — GDPR Right-to-Erasure and CCPA deletion requests are actually executable when they arrive.

SBS-DATA-002.pass_narrative: Your inventory of regulated-data fields is current, so you know exactly where personal data lives and can enforce protection, retention, and deletion obligations without scrambling to find it.

SBS-DATA-003.pass_narrative: Backups are tested, not just configured, so accidental deletion, malicious destruction, or configuration corruption can be recovered from with confidence rather than hope.

SBS-DATA-004.pass_narrative: Field history tracking is on for sensitive fields, so unauthorized or accidental changes are detectable, investigatable, and accountable rather than silent.

SBS-DEP-001.pass_narrative: Metadata changes go through a designated deployment identity, so production changes are attributable and unauthorized direct edits stand out instead of blending into routine admin activity.

SBS-DEP-002.pass_narrative: You've defined which metadata types are high-risk, so deployment governance has clear boundaries — Apex, auth settings, and outbound connectivity can't be edited directly in production.

SBS-DEP-003.pass_narrative: Unauthorized changes to high-risk metadata trigger alerts, so malicious or accidental drift gets surfaced quickly instead of persisting for weeks before being noticed.

SBS-DEP-004.pass_narrative: Production configuration ties back to a source-controlled approval trail, so 'what changed, when, by whom' is answerable — incident investigation has a real starting point instead of guesswork.

SBS-DEP-005.pass_narrative: Secret scanning runs on your repos, so hardcoded credentials get caught before they're committed — and the supply-chain path through repository access stays closed to outsiders.

SBS-DEP-006.pass_narrative: Your CLI Connected App has token expiration policies, so a stolen laptop's token files don't grant indefinite access to production — the credential exposure window is bounded.

SBS-INT-001.pass_narrative: Browser extensions accessing Salesforce go through governance, so cloned or malicious extensions can't quietly harvest session tokens or exfiltrate data from authenticated users.

SBS-INT-002.pass_narrative: Your Remote Site Settings inventory is documented with justification, so unvetted endpoints can't be authorized for Apex callouts and you know exactly which external services your code talks to.

SBS-INT-003.pass_narrative: Named Credentials are inventoried with justification, so undocumented integration paths can't quietly connect to insecure endpoints — every external connection is accounted for.

SBS-INT-004.pass_narrative: API Total Usage logs are retained for 30 days, so you have visibility into REST/SOAP/Bulk API activity — anomalous behavior is detectable and incident scope is actually determinable.

SBS-OAUTH-001.pass_narrative: Connected Apps go through formal installation, so security configuration — refresh token lifetimes, session policies, IP restrictions — is enforced by you, not inherited from the external developer.

SBS-OAUTH-002.pass_narrative: Connected Apps require explicit profile or permission set access, so a Connected App can't quietly authenticate any user — least-privilege actually applies to OAuth sessions.

SBS-OAUTH-003.pass_narrative: OAuth-enabled Connected Apps are classified by criticality, so risk assessment is anchored — you know which integrations touch sensitive data and where to focus governance and monitoring.

SBS-OAUTH-004.pass_narrative: High-risk Connected App vendors have documented due diligence on file, so onboarding decisions are informed and contractual obligations match the access being granted.

SBS-SECCONF-001.pass_narrative: You have a Health Check baseline, so configuration drift is actually detectable — you can tell when settings diverge from intentional decisions versus accumulated neglect.

SBS-SECCONF-002.pass_narrative: Health Check deviations get reviewed and remediated, so configuration drift doesn't quietly weaken your security posture between audits — your baseline stays meaningful over time.

SBS-CPORTAL-003.pass_narrative: Every Apex class and Flow exposed to your portal users is inventoried with documented profile access, so your external attack surface is knowable, security testing has scope, and unexpected externally-callable code can't accumulate quietly.

SBS-CPORTAL-004.pass_narrative: Portal-exposed Flows derive record access from the authenticated user's context, not from user-supplied input variables, so external users can't manipulate flow inputs to reach records they should never see.

SBS-CPORTAL-005.pass_narrative: Your portal security controls get exercised against adversarial input on a defined cadence, so configuration audits are corroborated by runtime testing — authorization gaps surface during testing instead of during a breach.

SBS-FDNS-001.pass_narrative: Your Salesforce security configurations, exceptions, justifications, and SBS-required inventories live in a centralized system of record, so governance survives staff turnover and audit conclusions can actually be reconstructed from durable artifacts.

SBS-FILE-001.pass_narrative: Public Content links carry expiry dates appropriate to the sensitivity of what they share, so a leaked or forgotten link doesn't extend exposure indefinitely — time-bounded access is governance you can actually enforce.

SBS-FILE-002.pass_narrative: Sensitive Public Content links are password-protected, so an intercepted or accidentally-shared URL alone isn't enough to view the content — there's an authentication layer between link possession and data exposure.

SBS-FILE-003.pass_narrative: Active Public Content links get reviewed and cleaned up on a defined cadence, so legacy and accidentally-shared links don't accumulate as forgotten exposure — your shared-content surface area stays bounded over time.

SBS-MON-001.pass_narrative: Event Monitoring log storage is enabled for the event types your security policy requires, so investigations have the telemetry to reconstruct what happened — file downloads, API calls, report exports, all retained instead of silently dropped.

SBS-MON-002.pass_narrative: Event logs persist for your required retention period, exported when Salesforce native retention falls short and protected from administrative deletion, so a slow-burn incident can still be reconstructed weeks or months after the fact.

SBS-MON-003.pass_narrative: Anomalous login patterns — impossible travel, suspicious networks, off-hours access, brute-force precursors — trigger investigation in your monitoring stack, so a compromised credential surfaces as an alert instead of a quiet foothold.

SBS-MON-004.pass_narrative: API activity is baselined and anomalies — mass exfiltration, unexpected object access, sudden write/delete shifts — surface as alerts, so post-authentication attacks via stolen tokens or compromised integrations don't operate silently.

SBS-MON-005.pass_narrative: API consumption is tracked against your daily limit with proactive alerts at 80-90% utilization, so a runaway integration or sudden spike gets triaged before the platform starts blocking calls and breaking core business processes.
